ehealth@who.int
Regarding a breach of patient privacy in Hong Kong
Dear Sir/Madam
I am writing in concern of a recent breach of patient privacy by the Hospital Authority (HA) of Hong Kong.

It was shown today in a reporters conference held by Former chairperson of the Hong Kong Public Doctors Association, Incumbent Legislative council member Dr. Pierre Chan, that the Hospital Authority (HA) of Hong Kong has provided the Hong Kong Police force with a backdoor into the HA clinical management system (CMS), allowing the police to access patient data without consent from either the patient or doctors - leading to police barging in to hospitals and arresting patients undergoing treatment.

This backdoor was used to target and arrest patients that were suspected to have participated in recent demonstrations against the government. This shows a clear breach in Patient Privacy, threatening not only the health of Hong Kong citizens,but also the trust of global citizens in any countries that are trying to develop its own eHealth system. Citizens around the world would have second thoughts in providing truthfully and factually correct information to their doctors, should they fear that their personal information could be used against them. Thus leading to inaccuracies in diagnosis, and thus, inaccurate treatment.

I humbly urge the WHO to look into the issue, and launch an investigation should any guidelines of the WHO be violated in this incident.

The following websites provide information to the issue
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bodIg9ySPKw
shows the reporters conference regarding the issue.

https://doctordaddysoccer.blogspot.com/2019/06/HA-AEIS.html?fbclid=IwAR3pJZ_QstUQoBXtodvNqGrZXA-44ieFnt6ZOBUt8JlScXI_17w12pp493E
Contains details of how the backdoor is accessed by the police force.

Sincerely,
A concerned Hong Kong Citizen

我proofread左一次
幫眼睇睇
Regarding a MAJOR breach of patient confidentiality in Hong Kong
Dear Sir/Madam

I am writing on behalf of the fellow citizen (請搵一堆人聯署）to express our profound concern regarding the breach of patient privacy by one of the most sizeable semi-governmental department, the Hospital Authority (HA) in Hong Kong.

In a press conference held by the former Chairperson of the Hong Kong Public Doctors Association, current member in the Legislative Council of Hong Kong, Dr. Pierre Chan stated that by creating (AEIS), anyone, including the Police Force, could access patient data related to recent protests, without any accounts or passwords. Thus creating a backdoor for the police to access patient data without the consent from patients, and the Hospital Authority has no idea of who have access to the data as well. This creates massive public concern regarding the recent arrest of protestors within the hospital premises, hence scaring some patients intended to seek help in public hospitals, delaying their treatments.

No matter what the intention was when creating the AEIS, this loophole in the system is believed to have been misused by the police force to target and arrest patients who were suspected to be protestors in the recent public demonstrations. This is a clear breach of patient privacy, breaching not only the trust of the public has towards the medical professionals, but also threatening the health of the citizens due to the mistrust developed in this incident.

In the age of health informatics, WHO plays an important role in advocating a safe eHealth system for countries around the globe. The current event in such a developed country in Hong Kong should draw global attention, with its action to be condemned. No one should be left out in the healthcare system, due to the fear of being arrested. Not everyone is born equal, but we can do our best to ensure we treat everyone equally.

I humbly urge the WHO to condemn the actions of the Hospital Authority and take necessary actions.

The following websites provide information to the issue

Regarding a MAJOR breach of patient data privacy in Hong Kong
Dear Sir/Madam

I am writing on behalf of the fellow citizen (請搵一堆人聯署）to express our profound concern regarding the breach of patient confidentiality by one of the most sizeable semi-governmental department, the Hospital Authority (HA) in Hong Kong.

In a press conference held by the former Chairperson of the Hong Kong Public Doctors Association, current member in the Legislative Council of Hong Kong, Dr. Pierre Chan stated that the informatic system used by AED contain a backdoor that allows access patient data related to recent protests, without any accounts or passwords. This creates massive public concern regarding the recent arrest of protestors within the hospital premises, hence scaring some patients who intended to seek help in public hospitals, delaying their treatments.

No matter what the intention was when creating the AEIS, this loophole in the system is believed to have been misused by the police force to target and arrest patients who were suspected to be protestors in the recent public demonstrations. This is a clear breach of patient data privacy, breaching not only the trust of the public has towards the medical professionals, but also threatening the health of the citizens by exploiting their right to seek proper healthcare.

In the age of health informatics, WHO plays an important role in advocating a safe eHealth system for countries around the globe. The current event in such a developed country in Hong Kong should draw global attention, with its action to be condemned. No one should be left out in the healthcare system, due to the fear of being arrested. Not everyone is born equal, but we can do our best to ensure we treat everyone equally.

I humbly urge the WHO to condemn the actions of the Hospital Authority and take necessary actions.

The following websites provide information to the issue

Regarding a MAJOR breach of patient data privacy in Hong Kong
Dear Sir/Madam

I am writing on behalf of the fellow citizen (請搵一堆人聯署）to express our profound concern regarding the breach of patient confidentiality by one of the most sizeable semi-governmental department, the Hospital Authority (HA) in Hong Kong.

In a press conference held by the former Chairperson of the Hong Kong Public Doctors Association, current member in the Legislative Council of Hong Kong, Dr. Pierre Chan stated that the informatic system used by AED contain a backdoor that allows access patient data related to recent protests, without any accounts or passwords. This has created massive public outrage regarding the recent arrest of protestors within hospital premises, hence scaring some patients who intended to seek help in public hospitals, delaying their treatments.

No matter what the intention was when creating the AEIS, this loophole in the system is believed to have been misused by the police force to target and arrest patients who were suspected to be protestors in the recent public demonstrations. This is a clear breach of patient data privacy, breaching not only the trust of the public has towards the medical professionals, but also threatening the health of the citizens by exploiting their right to seek proper healthcare.

In the age of bioinformatics, WHO plays an important role in advocating a safe eHealth system for countries around the globe. The current event in such a developed country in Hong Kong should draw global attention, with its action to be condemned. No one should be left out in the healthcare system, due to the fear of prosecution. Not everyone is born equal, but we can do our best to ensure we treat everyone equally.
